items.requirement: All California employers must maintain a written Injury and Illness Prevention Program (IIPP) addressing: (1) employer and employee responsibilities; (2) hazard identification and correction system; (3) safety communication system; (4) employee compliance procedures; (5) training; and (6) recordkeeping. The IIPP must be specific to this facility and must be kept on-site.
items.evidence_protocol: Request the written IIPP. Confirm: (1) it is site-specific, not a generic template; (2) it identifies a person responsible for implementing the program; (3) it includes a system for identifying and correcting hazards; (4) it includes an employee training program; (5) it is maintained and updated when hazards change. Sample 10% of employees for awareness of the IIPP.

items.requirement: When employees perform outdoor work with a heat index at or above 80°F, the employer must: (1) provide fresh, pure drinking water (1 qt/hour per employee); (2) provide access to shade when employees request it or when temperature exceeds 95°F (shade must accommodate all employees on a break simultaneously); (3) allow and encourage cool-down rest periods of at least 5 minutes when employee feels the need; (4) have emergency response procedures; (5) provide heat illness prevention training to employees and supervisors; (6) maintain a written Heat Illness Prevention Plan.
items.evidence_protocol: Request the written Heat Illness Prevention Plan. Verify it is specific to this facility and work conditions. Confirm: water supply is on-site and accessible; shade structures exist or portable shade is available; supervisors can demonstrate understanding of symptom recognition and first-response procedures for heat illness; training records exist for all employees working outdoors. Observe any outdoor work areas during audit.

items.requirement: Effective January 1, 2024: California's indoor heat illness prevention standard applies when indoor workplace temperatures reach or are expected to reach 82°F or above. Requirements include: (1) cool-down areas at a temperature below 82°F when indoor temps reach 82°F; (2) access to cool drinking water; (3) a written Indoor Heat Illness Prevention Plan; (4) training for all employees and supervisors; (5) acclimatization protocols for new and returning employees; (6) emergency response procedures.
items.evidence_protocol: Assess whether any indoor work areas may reach 82°F during operations (consider furnaces, compressors, foundry operations, boiler rooms, areas near heat-emitting processes). If applicable: request the written Indoor Heat Illness Prevention Plan; verify cool-down areas are designated and accessible; confirm water is available; verify training records. Ask supervisors to describe their procedure when a worker shows signs of heat illness.

items.requirement: When two or more employees performing the same or similar job tasks experience work-related repetitive motion injuries (RMIs) diagnosed by a licensed physician within a 12-month period, the employer must implement a Repetitive Motion Injury (RMI) Prevention Program. The program must include: (1) a worksite evaluation; (2) control of exposures that caused the RMIs; and (3) training of employees and supervisors.
items.evidence_protocol: Review OSHA 300 log and workers' compensation records for the past 12 months. Identify any RMI/ergonomic injuries (sprains, strains, carpal tunnel, tendinitis). If two or more employees in similar job tasks reported RMIs: confirm a written RMI prevention program exists; confirm worksite evaluations were conducted for the identified jobs; confirm engineering or administrative controls were implemented; verify training records. High-risk tasks to look for: repetitive hand/wrist motions, sustained awkward postures, heavy manual material handling.

items.requirement: Effective July 1, 2024 (SB 553): ALL California employers must: (1) develop and implement a written Workplace Violence Prevention Plan (WVPP); (2) establish and maintain a violent incident log; (3) provide training on the WVPP to employees; (4) review the WVPP after each violent incident or threat. The WVPP must include: hazard identification procedures, reporting procedures, incident response, post-incident review, and employee involvement.
items.evidence_protocol: Request the written Workplace Violence Prevention Plan. Confirm: it was developed after July 1, 2024; it identifies a person responsible for implementation; it includes a procedure for employees to report threats without fear of retaliation; a violent incident log is being maintained (even if no incidents have occurred, the log must exist); training was provided to all employees and documented. Ask a random sample of 3 employees: 'Do you know how to report a threat or violent incident?'

items.requirement: California employers must use Cal/OSHA-specific recordkeeping forms (Cal/OSHA Form 300, 300A, 301) which are identical in format to federal forms. Establishments with 250+ employees OR establishments in NAICS codes identified by Cal/OSHA must electronically submit Form 300A data annually. The Cal/OSHA 300 log must be posted from February 1 through April 30 each year.
items.evidence_protocol: Request the facility's Cal/OSHA 300 log and 300A summary. Confirm Cal/OSHA-specific forms are used (not just the federal OSHA form). For facilities with 250+ employees: confirm electronic submission was completed for the most recent calendar year; request submission confirmation documentation. Verify 300A is posted during the February–April window (check for prior year posted copy).

items.requirement: California's Hazardous Substances Information and Training Act (Labor Code §6360 et seq.) requires that employers provide employees with information on hazardous substances present in the workplace, supplementing federal HazCom. Additionally, Proposition 65 (Health & Safety Code §25249.6) requires businesses with 10+ employees to provide 'clear and reasonable warnings' before knowingly exposing anyone to chemicals on the Proposition 65 list at workplace locations where exposure occurs.
items.evidence_protocol: Verify that SDS/training programs cover all California-listed hazardous substances (verify against California Hazardous Substance List). For facilities with 10+ employees: identify any Proposition 65 listed chemicals present on-site (common examples: lead, benzene, crystalline silica, diesel engine exhaust, formaldehyde, chromium compounds); confirm Prop 65 warning signs are posted at areas where exposure may occur if the chemical is present above the NSRL. Request a Prop 65 exposure assessment if none exists.

items.requirement: California OSHA has established Permissible Exposure Limits (PELs) that are more stringent than federal OSHA for several substances. California's PEL table (Title 8 CCR §5155, Table AC-1) contains PELs for substances not regulated at the federal level and lower limits for regulated substances. Any chemical present in the workplace must be evaluated against California's Table AC-1, not just federal OSHA's Z-1 table.
items.evidence_protocol: Obtain the facility's industrial hygiene exposure data (monitoring records, third-party IH assessments) for the past 3 years. Compare any measured air contaminant concentrations against California Table AC-1 limits, not just federal PELs. Priority chemicals to verify: silica (Cal/OSHA has a distinct PEL at 0.1 mg/m3 RCS), lead (more stringent action level), asbestos, benzene. Confirm the facility's IH program uses California PELs as the compliance benchmark.

items.requirement: California's confined space standard (Title 8 CCR §5157) is substantively equivalent to federal 29 CFR 1910.146 but Cal/OSHA enforcement historically requires more detailed permit documentation. Cal/OSHA inspectors frequently cite facilities for: atmospheric monitoring data not recorded with actual readings on the permit, attendant and supervisor names not signed on the permit, and rescue equipment not matching the specific space hazard profile.
items.evidence_protocol: Review 10% of canceled confined space entry permits from the past 12 months. In addition to the 15 required federal elements, verify: actual atmospheric readings (numbers, not checkmarks) are recorded; the instrument type and calibration date are documented on or with the permit; the entry supervisor signed the permit before entry was authorized (not after); rescue service contact info is a specific phone number, not 'call 911'. Compare against federal CS audit findings to identify any Cal/OSHA-specific gaps.

items.requirement: California employers must display a specific set of required posters and notices that supplement federal posting requirements. Missing or outdated Cal/OSHA and state labor postings are routinely cited during inspections.
items.evidence_protocol: Confirm the following California-required postings are current, legible, and posted in a conspicuous location where all employees can access them: (1) Cal/OSHA 'Safety and Health Protection on the Job' poster; (2) IWC Wage Order for the applicable industry (e.g., IWC Order 1 for manufacturing); (3) Notice to Employees — Workers' Compensation; (4) Paid Sick Leave notice; (5) DFEH poster on employment discrimination; (6) Payday Notice. Confirm all postings are current versions (check publication year on each poster).

items.requirement: All Michigan employers must develop, maintain, and administer a written Safety and Health Program. The program must address: (1) goals and objectives; (2) identification of persons responsible for safety; (3) procedures for identifying and correcting hazards; (4) employee training; and (5) a system for encouraging employee reporting of hazards. The program must be made available to employees.
items.evidence_protocol: Request the facility's written Safety and Health Program. Confirm: it is site-specific (not a generic template); a named individual is designated as responsible; a hazard identification/correction process is documented; training is addressed; employees can access the program. Interview 2–3 employees to verify awareness of the program and the designated safety contact.

items.requirement: Michigan requires employers to report all workplace fatalities and any in-patient hospitalization, amputation, or loss of an eye to MIOSHA within 24 hours of learning of the incident. This mirrors federal OSHA thresholds. Confirm the facility knows the correct MIOSHA reporting contact and has it accessible to supervisors.
items.evidence_protocol: Verify that supervisors and the EHS/safety function know the MIOSHA emergency reporting hotline and reporting obligation. Confirm reporting thresholds are documented in the EAP or similar procedure: fatality or imminent-danger hospitalization within 8 hours; in-patient hospitalization/amputation/eye loss within 24 hours. Review any incidents in the past 12 months and confirm required notifications were made.

items.requirement: Michigan employers must display the Michigan OSHA (MIOSHA) 'Michigan Safety and Health Programs' poster in a conspicuous location accessible to employees. This is a Michigan-specific poster that supplements but does not replace the federal OSHA poster.
items.evidence_protocol: Confirm the MIOSHA-specific poster is posted at each facility entrance or primary employee gathering area. The poster must be current (check publication year). Confirm the federal OSHA poster is also posted (both are required). MIOSHA posters are available at no charge from MIOSHA at michigan.gov/leo/miosha.

items.requirement: Michigan's Employee Right-to-Know rules supplement federal HazCom. Michigan employees have the right to: (1) obtain a copy of any MSDS/SDS for any chemical they are exposed to; (2) see exposure records; (3) receive information about the identity and health effects of hazardous substances; (4) access the facility's chemical inventory. The employer must not interfere with the exercise of these rights.
items.evidence_protocol: Confirm that the facility's HazCom program includes a documented process for employees to request and receive SDS copies. Verify SDSs are accessible during all work shifts (not locked in a supervisor's office). Interview 2–3 employees: 'If you wanted to see the safety data sheet for a chemical you use, how would you get it?' Confirm the chemical inventory is current and accessible.

items.requirement: During a MIOSHA inspection, employees have the right to have an authorized employee representative accompany the MIOSHA compliance officer on the walkaround. The employer must notify employees of a MIOSHA inspection and cannot retaliate against employees who cooperate with MIOSHA or who report workplace hazards.
items.evidence_protocol: Confirm that supervisors and the EHS function are aware of employee walkaround rights during MIOSHA inspections. Review the facility's response procedures for MIOSHA inspections. Confirm no adverse actions were taken against employees who reported safety concerns or participated in prior MIOSHA inspections (review any MIOSHA discrimination complaints or internal corrective actions in prior 12 months).

items.requirement: Michigan requires that at least one employee trained in first aid and CPR is available on each work shift at locations where medical services are not available within a reasonable proximity. Training must be provided at no cost to the employee and must be kept current.
items.evidence_protocol: Confirm the number of employees trained in first aid/CPR per shift. Verify: current training records exist; training was provided at employer expense; the nearest hospital or emergency medical service response time has been evaluated to determine whether on-site first aid capability is required. At manufacturing sites, if response time exceeds 3–4 minutes, trained first aid personnel on each shift is expected.

items.requirement: All Washington employers must develop and implement a written Accident Prevention Program (APP). The APP must address: (1) management safety commitment; (2) employee involvement; (3) hazard identification and control; (4) safety training for each employee; (5) incident investigation; and (6) program evaluation. The APP must be specific to the operations at this facility.
items.evidence_protocol: Request the written Accident Prevention Program. Confirm: the APP is signed or endorsed by senior site management; it covers the specific operations and hazards at this facility; hazard identification procedures (periodic inspections, employee reporting) are documented; incident investigation procedures are included; training requirements are specified. Sample 3 employees to verify they are aware of the APP and how to report hazards.

items.requirement: Washington employers with 11 or more employees must establish a safety committee with equal employer and employee representation. Safety committee meetings must be held at least monthly and minutes must be documented and retained for at least 3 years. All employers must conduct safety meetings at least quarterly, regardless of size.
items.evidence_protocol: For facilities with 11+ employees: confirm a joint labor-management safety committee exists; verify meeting frequency is at least monthly; review meeting minutes for the past 12 months and confirm they document hazards identified, corrective actions, and follow-up. For all facilities: confirm safety meetings are held at least quarterly and documented. Ask to see the most recent 3 sets of meeting minutes.

items.requirement: Washington's Ergonomics Rule requires employers to identify and control Caution Zone Jobs (high hand forces, highly repetitive motions, awkward postures) and Action Level Jobs (higher-intensity combinations of risk factors). When Action Level jobs are identified, employers must implement engineering controls or work practice controls and provide training.
items.evidence_protocol: Request any ergonomic job analysis or MSD risk assessment conducted at the facility. Review OSHA 300 log for MSD-type injuries (sprains, strains, tendinitis, carpal tunnel). Identify the 3 highest-MSD-risk job tasks. For each: determine whether the task meets Washington Caution Zone or Action Level criteria; confirm controls (engineering, administrative) are in place; verify ergonomics training is provided. Document any unaddressed action-level jobs.

items.requirement: Washington employers must display the L&I 'Job Safety and Health Law' poster and any applicable industrial insurance (workers' compensation) poster. Washington-specific postings supplement but do not replace the federal OSHA poster.
items.evidence_protocol: Confirm the Washington L&I safety and health poster is posted in a location accessible to all employees. Confirm the Washington workers' compensation employer information is posted. Confirm the federal OSHA poster is also present. Check publication dates on all posters to confirm they are current versions.

items.requirement: Washington requires employers to report any workplace injury or illness requiring in-patient hospitalization within 8 hours of learning of the incident. This is more stringent than the federal OSHA 24-hour window for hospitalizations. Fatalities must also be reported within 8 hours.
items.evidence_protocol: Confirm that site supervisors and the EHS function know the 8-hour reporting window for hospitalizations (not the federal 24-hour window). Verify the L&I reporting procedure is documented and accessible to supervisors on all shifts. Review any incidents in the past 12 months involving hospitalization and confirm the 8-hour notification was made to L&I.

items.requirement: Washington employees have the statutory right to refuse work they reasonably believe presents an imminent danger to their health or safety. Employers cannot discipline or terminate employees for good-faith refusals to perform imminently dangerous work. The employer must investigate the hazard and address it before requiring the work to be performed.
items.evidence_protocol: Confirm the facility has a documented procedure for employees to report hazards and refuse imminently dangerous work. Review any prior worker complaints to L&I or internal safety concern reports from the past 24 months. Interview 3 employees: 'What would you do if you were asked to do something you believed was dangerous?' Confirm no adverse employment actions were taken related to safety refusals.

items.requirement: Washington establishments with 250 or more employees (or establishments in high-hazard NAICS codes as defined by L&I) must electronically submit OSHA Form 300A summary data to the federal OSHA electronic reporting system annually by March 2. Washington follows the federal Injury Tracking Application (ITA) requirement.
items.evidence_protocol: For facilities meeting the 250-employee or high-hazard NAICS threshold: confirm electronic submission of OSHA 300A data was completed for the most recent calendar year by March 2; request submission confirmation or ITA dashboard screenshot. Confirm the correct establishment ID is on file with the ITA system.

items.requirement: All Oregon employers must have a written Accident Prevention Program (APP). Employers with 11 or more employees or in high-hazard industries must also have a functioning safety committee. The APP must address: hazard identification, hazard correction, accident investigation, training, and employee involvement.
items.evidence_protocol: Request the written Accident Prevention Program. Confirm: it is site-specific; management commitment is documented; hazard identification procedures are included; incident investigation procedures are present; training requirements are addressed. For facilities with 11+ employees: confirm a safety committee exists and is functioning (request meeting minutes for the past 12 months).

items.requirement: Oregon employers with 11 or more employees must maintain a safety committee meeting at least quarterly. Smaller employers must conduct safety meetings at least monthly. Minutes must be documented and retained for 3 years. Safety committee members must include an equal number of employer and employee representatives.
items.evidence_protocol: For facilities with 11+ employees: request safety committee minutes for the past 12 months; confirm meeting frequency is at least quarterly; confirm minutes document topics, attendees, hazards identified, and corrective actions. For all facilities: confirm safety meetings occur at required frequency and documentation is maintained.

items.requirement: Oregon's ergonomics rule requires employers to address musculoskeletal disorder (MSD) hazards. When MSDs are occurring or when MSD risk factors are present, employers must analyze the work tasks contributing to the risk and implement feasible engineering, administrative, or work practice controls. Training must be provided to employees performing at-risk tasks.
items.evidence_protocol: Review OSHA 300 log for MSD injuries (sprains, strains, tendinitis, carpal tunnel, rotator cuff injuries). For each MSD event or high-risk task identified: confirm a job hazard analysis or ergonomic assessment was conducted; confirm controls were evaluated and implemented where feasible; verify MSD prevention training was provided. Document any unaddressed MSD hazards.

items.requirement: Oregon employers must display the Oregon OSHA 'Safety and Health on the Job' poster and the Oregon workers' compensation insurance poster in locations accessible to all employees.
items.evidence_protocol: Confirm the Oregon OSHA poster is posted (separate from the federal OSHA poster). Confirm the Oregon workers' compensation poster is displayed. Verify both postings are current versions. Posters are available at osha.oregon.gov.

items.requirement: Oregon employers must report workplace fatalities within 8 hours and any in-patient hospitalization within 24 hours of learning of the incident. Confirm the facility has the Oregon OSHA reporting contact information accessible to supervisors on all shifts.
items.evidence_protocol: Confirm that supervisors know the Oregon OSHA reporting obligation and the 24-hour window for hospitalizations. The OR-OSHA emergency reporting line is (503) 378-3272. Review any hospitalizations in the past 12 months and verify notifications were made. Confirm the reporting procedure is documented in the EAP or emergency response plan.

items.requirement: All Minnesota employers must maintain a written safety program addressing: (1) a management statement of safety commitment; (2) specific safety goals and objectives; (3) assignment of safety responsibilities; (4) procedures for identifying and correcting hazards; (5) employee training requirements; and (6) a system for reviewing and updating the program. The program must be available to employees.
items.evidence_protocol: Request the written safety program. Confirm: a signed management commitment statement is present; responsibilities are assigned to specific positions or individuals; a hazard identification and correction procedure is documented; training requirements are specified; a review/update schedule exists. Interview 2–3 employees to confirm they are aware of the safety program and safety goals.

items.requirement: Minnesota employers with 25 or more employees in manufacturing or construction must maintain a joint labor-management safety committee. The committee must meet at least monthly and keep minutes of all meetings. Committee responsibilities include reviewing safety reports, inspecting the workplace, recommending corrective actions, and evaluating safety program effectiveness.
items.evidence_protocol: For manufacturing facilities with 25+ employees: confirm the safety committee exists with equal employer and employee representation; request meeting minutes for the past 12 months; confirm meeting frequency is monthly; verify the committee reviews safety data (incident reports, inspection results) and that corrective action recommendations are documented and followed up.

items.requirement: The Minnesota Employee Right-to-Know Act supplements federal HazCom. Minnesota employers must: (1) provide employees with a list of all hazardous substances present in the workplace upon request; (2) ensure SDSs are accessible; (3) provide training on specific chemicals present at this facility. The Right-to-Know list must include all chemicals on the Minnesota Hazardous Substances List.
items.evidence_protocol: Confirm the facility maintains a current chemical inventory accessible to employees. Verify a procedure exists for employees to request the list and to access SDSs at any time. Review HazCom training to confirm training addresses specific chemicals present at this facility, not just generic HazCom content. Interview 2 employees: 'Do you know how to get a list of all the hazardous chemicals at this site?'

items.requirement: Minnesota employers must display the MN OSHA 'Safety and Health Protection on the Job' poster. Additional Minnesota-required postings include: the Minnesota Minimum Wage notice, the Workers' Compensation poster, and the Equal Pay/Equal Employment Opportunity notice.
items.evidence_protocol: Confirm the Minnesota OSHA poster is posted in a conspicuous location. Confirm the federal OSHA poster is also present. Verify additional Minnesota-required postings are current (Minimum Wage, Workers' Comp, EEOC). Check publication dates on all posters. Posters are available at dli.mn.gov.
items.citation: MN Rules 5205.0030; MN Stat. §177.23; MN Stat. §176.138

items.requirement: Minnesota follows federal OSHA recordkeeping requirements (OSHA 300/300A/301). Electronic submission of 300A data is required annually for establishments with 250+ employees or those in high-hazard NAICS codes. MN OSHA may request the full 300 log and supporting records at any time and employers must provide access within 4 business hours.
items.evidence_protocol: For facilities with 250+ employees or in applicable NAICS: confirm electronic submission of 300A to federal ITA system was completed for the most recent calendar year. For all facilities: confirm OSHA 300/301 records are maintained and accessible. Confirm the 300A was posted February 1–April 30. Confirm records are retained for 5 years.

items.requirement: Indiana adopts the 2012 International Fire Code (IFC) through 675 IAC 22-2.5. This requires full compliance with NFPA 10 (Standard for Portable Fire Extinguishers), which is more stringent than federal OSHA 1910.157. NFPA 10 requires: (1) a 6-year internal inspection (teardown) of all stored-pressure fire extinguishers; (2) hydrostatic testing on a type-specific schedule (stored-pressure dry chemical: every 12 years; CO2: every 5 years); (3) annual maintenance must be performed by a qualified person and documented with a collar tag and maintenance record. Indiana fire code is enforced by the Indiana Fire Marshal, separate from and in addition to IOSHA.
items.evidence_protocol: Review fire extinguisher inspection and maintenance records. Confirm: annual maintenance records exist for all extinguishers with collar tags attached showing date and technician; for stored-pressure dry chemical extinguishers, confirm 6-year internal inspection has been completed (check tag history); for CO2 extinguishers, verify 5-year hydrostatic testing. Confirm the facility tracks due dates for internal inspections and hydrostatic testing by extinguisher ID. Note any extinguishers with expired testing requirements.

items.requirement: Under the Indiana Fire Code (IFC Section 2703.1), facilities that store or use hazardous materials above maximum allowable quantities (MAQ) listed in IFC Tables 2703.1.1(1) and (2) may be required to prepare a Hazardous Materials Management Plan (HMMP) and Hazardous Materials Inventory Statement (HMIS). These documents must be submitted to and maintained with the local fire marshal.
items.evidence_protocol: Review the facility's hazardous materials inventory and compare stored quantities against IFC Table 2703.1.1 MAQ thresholds for each hazardous material category (flammable liquids, compressed gases, oxidizers, corrosives, etc.). If quantities exceed control area MAQs: confirm an HMMP has been prepared; confirm the HMMP has been submitted to the local fire authority having jurisdiction (AHJ); verify a copy is on file at the facility and maintained current.

items.requirement: Indiana's adoption of the International Fire Code requires a continuous fire watch for a minimum of 60 minutes after the completion of hot work operations in any area where combustible materials may be present. Federal OSHA 29 CFR 1910.252(a)(2)(iv) recommends but does not codify a specific fire watch duration.
items.evidence_protocol: Review the facility's hot work permit program. Confirm the written hot work procedure specifies a minimum 60-minute fire watch after work completion. Review 10% of completed hot work permits from the past 12 months (minimum 3) and confirm the fire watch end time documented on each permit reflects at least 60 minutes post-completion. Confirm fire watch personnel are trained in the scope of their duties and how to report a fire.

items.requirement: Indiana employers must display the Indiana IOSHA poster ('Indiana Safety and Health Laws') in addition to the federal OSHA poster. The IOSHA-specific poster informs employees of their rights under the Indiana Occupational Safety and Health Act.
items.evidence_protocol: Confirm the Indiana IOSHA-specific poster is posted in a conspicuous location accessible to all employees. Confirm the federal OSHA poster is also present. Verify the Indiana poster is a current version. Posters are available at in.gov/dol/iosha.

items.requirement: North Carolina employers must display the North Carolina Department of Labor 'North Carolina Occupational Safety and Health Act' poster in addition to the federal OSHA poster.
items.evidence_protocol: Confirm the NC DOL OSH-specific poster is posted and current. Confirm the federal OSHA poster is also present. NC DOL posters are available at nclabor.com. Verify the poster is posted in a location visible and accessible to all employees.

items.requirement: North Carolina requires reporting of fatalities within 8 hours and in-patient hospitalizations, amputations, and eye loss within 24 hours to the NC Department of Labor OSH Division. Confirm the facility has the NC DOL OSH reporting contact information accessible.
items.evidence_protocol: Confirm the NC DOL OSH emergency reporting number is documented and accessible to supervisors on all shifts. The NC DOL OSH reporting line is (919) 807-2900. Review any fatality or serious injury events in the past 12 months and confirm required notifications were made to NC DOL OSH.

items.requirement: North Carolina adopts federal OSHA General Industry standards by reference through 13 NCAC 07F. NC OSH enforces standards substantively equivalent to federal 29 CFR 1910. NC OSH may have supplemental rules for specific industries; verify current NC DOL OSH standards at nclabor.com at the time of this audit for any industry-specific additions.
items.evidence_protocol: Confirm compliance with all applicable federal OSHA GI standards (primary audit tabs). Verify the EHS responsible party has checked nclabor.com for any NC-specific amendments effective at the time of this audit. Document the date of verification and standard set reviewed.

items.requirement: Virginia employers must display the Virginia OSHA (VOSH) 'Job Safety and Health Protection' poster in addition to the federal OSHA poster.
items.evidence_protocol: Confirm the VOSH-specific poster is posted and current. Confirm the federal OSHA poster is also present. Verify the poster is in a location visible to all employees. VOSH posters are available at doli.virginia.gov.

items.requirement: VOSH requires reporting of fatalities within 8 hours and in-patient hospitalizations within 24 hours. VOSH also requires reporting of any hospitalization, amputation, or eye loss within 24 hours of learning of the incident.
items.evidence_protocol: Confirm the VOSH reporting contact information is accessible to supervisors. The VOSH emergency reporting number is (540) 562-3580. Review any fatality or serious injury incidents in the past 12 months and verify required notifications were made within required timeframes.

items.requirement: Virginia adopts federal OSHA general industry standards through 16 VAC 25-90. VOSH may adopt amendments or additional standards; verify the current VOSH General Industry Standards at doli.virginia.gov at time of audit.
items.evidence_protocol: Confirm compliance with all applicable federal OSHA GI standards. Verify the EHS responsible party has reviewed 16 VAC 25-90 for any current VOSH-specific amendments. Document the date of standard verification.

items.requirement: South Carolina employers must display the SC OSH 'Job Safety and Health: It's the Law' poster in addition to the federal OSHA poster.
items.evidence_protocol: Confirm the SC OSH-specific poster is posted and current. Confirm the federal OSHA poster is also present. SC OSH posters are available at labor.sc.gov/safety. Verify both are in a location accessible to all employees.

items.requirement: South Carolina OSH requires reporting of fatalities within 8 hours and in-patient hospitalizations, amputations, and eye loss within 24 hours to SC OSHA. Confirm the facility has SC OSH emergency reporting contact information.
items.evidence_protocol: Confirm the SC OSH reporting contact information is accessible to supervisors. Review any fatality or serious injury events in the past 12 months and verify required notifications were made. The SC OSH reporting line is (803) 896-4300.

items.requirement: South Carolina adopts federal OSHA General Industry standards by reference. Verify current SC OSH standards at labor.sc.gov at time of audit for any SC-specific amendments in effect.
items.evidence_protocol: Confirm compliance with all applicable federal OSHA GI standards. Verify that the EHS function has checked labor.sc.gov for current SC OSHA amendments effective at time of this audit.

items.requirement: Alaska employers must display the Alaska OSHA 'Safety and Health Protection on the Job' poster in addition to the federal OSHA poster.
items.evidence_protocol: Confirm the Alaska OSHA-specific poster is posted and current. Confirm the federal OSHA poster is also present. AK OSHA posters are available at labor.alaska.gov/lss/oshhome.htm.

items.requirement: Alaska's unique climate presents occupational cold stress hazards during outdoor and semi-outdoor work. Alaska OSHA addresses temperature extremes in 8 AAC 61.1200. Employers with employees working in cold environments must implement cold stress prevention measures: acclimatization for new workers, buddy system protocols, warm shelter access, PPE adequate for temperature and wind chill, and training on cold injury recognition and prevention.
items.evidence_protocol: Assess whether any work is performed outdoors or in unheated spaces during cold weather months. If applicable: confirm the facility has a cold stress prevention procedure addressing temperature and wind chill thresholds; verify warm break areas are accessible; confirm employees are trained on hypothermia, frostbite, and cold emergency response. For extreme cold operations (below 0°F): verify PPE (insulated clothing, hand protection, face protection) is provided and appropriate for temperature.

items.requirement: Alaska follows federal OSHA reporting thresholds: fatalities within 8 hours and in-patient hospitalizations, amputations, or eye loss within 24 hours. Confirm the facility has the AK OSHA emergency reporting contact accessible.
items.evidence_protocol: Confirm the AK OSHA reporting contact is documented and accessible. The AK OSHA reporting number is (907) 269-4957. Review any fatality or serious injury events in the past 12 months and verify required notifications were made.

items.requirement: Alaska adopts federal OSHA General Industry standards through 8 AAC 61. Verify current AK OSHA standards at labor.alaska.gov at time of audit for any Alaska-specific amendments.
items.evidence_protocol: Confirm compliance with all applicable federal OSHA GI standards. Verify that the EHS function has reviewed 8 AAC 61 for current AK OSHA-specific amendments effective at time of this audit.

items.requirement: Arizona employers must display the Arizona Industrial Commission 'Arizona Occupational Safety and Health' poster in addition to the federal OSHA poster.
items.evidence_protocol: Confirm the AZ OSHA-specific poster is posted and current. Confirm the federal OSHA poster is also present. AZ OSHA posters are available at azica.gov/labor-arizona-occupational-safety-and-health-division. Verify both are accessible to all employees.

items.requirement: Arizona follows federal OSHA reporting thresholds. Confirm the facility has the AZ OSHA emergency reporting contact accessible to supervisors.
items.evidence_protocol: Confirm the AZ OSHA reporting contact is documented. The AZ OSHA reporting line is (602) 542-5795 or 1-800-545-5525. Review any serious injury events in the past 12 months and verify required notifications were made within required timeframes.

items.requirement: Arizona adopts federal OSHA General Industry standards. Verify current AZ OSHA standards at azica.gov at time of audit for any Arizona-specific amendments.
items.evidence_protocol: Confirm compliance with all applicable federal OSHA GI standards. Verify that the EHS function has checked azica.gov for current AZ OSHA amendments effective at time of this audit.

items.requirement: Hawaii employers must display the Hawaii HIOSH 'Occupational Safety and Health' poster in addition to the federal OSHA poster.
items.evidence_protocol: Confirm the Hawaii HIOSH-specific poster is posted and current. Confirm the federal OSHA poster is also present. HIOSH posters are available at labor.hawaii.gov/hiosh. Verify both are in a location accessible to all employees.

items.requirement: Hawaii follows federal OSHA reporting thresholds. Confirm the facility has the HIOSH emergency reporting contact accessible to supervisors.
items.evidence_protocol: Confirm the HIOSH reporting contact is documented and accessible. The HIOSH reporting line is (808) 586-9116. Review any serious injury events in the past 12 months and verify required notifications were made.

items.requirement: Hawaii adopts federal OSHA General Industry standards through HAR §12-110. Certain industry-specific rules may apply. Verify current HIOSH standards at labor.hawaii.gov at time of audit.
items.evidence_protocol: Confirm compliance with all applicable federal OSHA GI standards. Verify that the EHS function has checked labor.hawaii.gov/hiosh for current Hawaii-specific standards or amendments effective at time of this audit.

items.requirement: Iowa employers must display the Iowa OSHA 'Job Safety and Health Protection' poster in addition to the federal OSHA poster.
items.evidence_protocol: Confirm the Iowa OSHA-specific poster is posted and current. Confirm the federal OSHA poster is also present. Iowa OSHA posters are available at iowaosha.gov. Verify both are accessible to all employees.

items.requirement: Iowa follows federal OSHA reporting thresholds. Confirm the facility has the Iowa OSHA emergency reporting contact accessible to supervisors.
items.evidence_protocol: Confirm the Iowa OSHA reporting contact is documented and accessible. Review any serious injury events in the past 12 months and verify required notifications were made within required timeframes.

items.requirement: Iowa adopts federal OSHA General Industry standards. Verify current Iowa OSHA standards at iowaosha.gov at time of audit for any Iowa-specific amendments.
items.evidence_protocol: Confirm compliance with all applicable federal OSHA GI standards. Verify that the EHS function has reviewed iowaosha.gov for current Iowa-specific amendments effective at time of this audit.
items.citation: Iowa Admin. Code 875; Iowa Code §88 et seq.

items.requirement: Kentucky employers must display the Kentucky Labor Cabinet OSHA poster in addition to the federal OSHA poster.
items.evidence_protocol: Confirm the Kentucky Labor Cabinet OSHA-specific poster is posted and current. Confirm the federal OSHA poster is also present. KY OSH posters are available at labor.ky.gov. Verify both are accessible to all employees.

items.requirement: Kentucky follows federal OSHA reporting thresholds. Confirm the facility has the KY OSH emergency reporting contact accessible to supervisors.
items.evidence_protocol: Confirm the KY OSH reporting contact is documented and accessible. The KY OSH reporting line is (502) 564-3070. Review any serious injury events in the past 12 months and verify required notifications were made.

items.requirement: Kentucky adopts federal OSHA General Industry standards by reference. Verify current KY OSH standards at labor.ky.gov at time of audit for any Kentucky-specific amendments.
items.evidence_protocol: Confirm compliance with all applicable federal OSHA GI standards. Verify that the EHS function has reviewed labor.ky.gov for current KY OSH-specific amendments effective at time of this audit.

items.requirement: Maryland employers must display the Maryland OSHA (MOSH) 'Job Safety and Health Protection' poster in addition to the federal OSHA poster.
items.evidence_protocol: Confirm the MOSH-specific poster is posted and current. Confirm the federal OSHA poster is also present. MOSH posters are available at dllr.state.md.us/labor/mosh. Verify both are in a location accessible to all employees.

items.requirement: Maryland follows federal OSHA reporting thresholds. Confirm the facility has the MOSH emergency reporting contact accessible to supervisors.
items.evidence_protocol: Confirm the MOSH reporting contact is documented. The MOSH emergency line is (410) 527-4447. Review any serious injury events in the past 12 months and verify required notifications were made.

items.requirement: Maryland MOSH historically enforces the engineering controls hierarchy in 29 CFR 1910.95 more aggressively than federal OSHA. If noise levels exceed 90 dBA TWA, MOSH expects documented evidence that feasible engineering controls were evaluated and either implemented or documented as infeasible before relying solely on hearing protection.
items.evidence_protocol: Review noise monitoring data. For any tasks where noise exceeds 90 dBA TWA: confirm a documented evaluation of engineering controls (machine enclosures, isolation, vibration dampening, process changes) was conducted; confirm controls were implemented where feasible; where engineering controls were deemed infeasible, confirm written documentation of the infeasibility determination. Confirm hearing protection is not the first and only control.

items.requirement: Maryland adopts federal OSHA General Industry standards. Verify current MOSH standards at dllr.state.md.us at time of audit for any Maryland-specific amendments.
items.evidence_protocol: Confirm compliance with all applicable federal OSHA GI standards. Verify that the EHS function has reviewed COMAR 09.12 for current MOSH-specific amendments effective at time of this audit.
items.citation: COMAR 09.12; MD Code, Labor & Empl. §5-101 et seq.

items.requirement: Nevada employers must display the Nevada OSHA 'Nevada Job Safety and Health Protection' poster in addition to the federal OSHA poster.
items.evidence_protocol: Confirm the Nevada OSHA-specific poster is posted and current. Confirm the federal OSHA poster is also present. NV OSHA posters are available at business.nv.gov/osha. Verify both are accessible to all employees.

items.requirement: Nevada follows federal OSHA reporting thresholds. Confirm the facility has the Nevada OSHA emergency reporting contact accessible to supervisors.
items.evidence_protocol: Confirm the Nevada OSHA reporting contact is documented and accessible. The NV OSHA reporting line is (775) 688-3700 (northern NV) or (702) 486-9020 (southern NV). Review any serious injury events in the past 12 months and verify required notifications were made.

items.requirement: Nevada adopts federal OSHA General Industry standards by reference. Verify current NV OSHA standards at business.nv.gov at time of audit for any Nevada-specific amendments.
items.evidence_protocol: Confirm compliance with all applicable federal OSHA GI standards. Verify that the EHS function has reviewed NAC 618 for current NV OSHA-specific amendments effective at time of this audit.

items.requirement: Puerto Rico employers must display the PR OSHA poster in BOTH English and Spanish in locations accessible to all employees.
items.evidence_protocol: Confirm the bilingual (English and Spanish) PR OSHA poster is posted and current. Confirm the federal OSHA poster is also present. Verify both language versions are legible and posted in locations accessible to all employees.
items.citation: PR Law 16 Art. 5; PR OSH Act

items.requirement: Puerto Rico OSH requires that safety training, safety communications, and required written safety programs be provided in Spanish — the primary language of the workforce. All safety programs, permits, and procedures must be available in Spanish for employee use. Employers must also ensure that training is comprehensible to employees in their primary language.
items.evidence_protocol: Review all safety training materials, programs, and procedures used at this facility. Confirm: all required written safety programs are available in Spanish; training is conducted in Spanish or with bilingual trainers and bilingual materials; safety signs and labels in work areas are in Spanish; confirm employee understanding through interviews in Spanish with a sample of 3 employees.
items.citation: PR OSH Act Art. 18; PR Law 16

items.requirement: Puerto Rico follows federal OSHA reporting thresholds. PR OSHA is administered by the Puerto Rico Department of Labor and Human Resources. Confirm the facility has PR OSHA emergency reporting contact accessible.
items.evidence_protocol: Confirm the PR OSHA reporting contact is documented and accessible. Review any serious injury events in the past 12 months and verify required notifications were made within required timeframes. Confirm the site's understanding that PR OSHA — not federal OSHA — has jurisdiction.
items.citation: PR OSH Act Art. 14; PR Law 16

items.requirement: Puerto Rico adopts federal OSHA General Industry standards. Verify current PR OSHA standards at labor.pr.gov at time of audit for any Puerto Rico-specific amendments.
items.evidence_protocol: Confirm compliance with all applicable federal OSHA GI standards. Verify that the EHS function has reviewed PR OSHA rules at labor.pr.gov for current PR-specific amendments effective at time of this audit.
